query: mechanism of action of ionizing radiation on cancer cells

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction -- Physical and Biological Principles of Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation takes advantage of cancer cells because these cells usually have impaired DNA repair mechanisms, in contrast to normal cells, which can rapidly repair double-stranded breaks. Therefore, a fractionated approach, i.e., splitting the total radiation dose over multiple daily treatments, is typically used, such that DNA damage in normal cells is repaired between treatments, while damage to cancer cells accumulates over time, causing preferential cancer cell death.  Both the dose per fraction and the total dose affect tumor and normal tissue response. In general, the lower the daily dose of radiation, the less likely it is to cause toxicity, but only specific cell lines (such as myeloma or lymphoma) are susceptible to these relatively low daily doses. Therefore, a balance needs to be achieved between daily doses low enough to spare normal tissue but high enough to cause cancer cell death. For many cancers, a dose of 180 to 200 cGy per day is used. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The principles of radiation physics apply to the field of radiation oncology, and ionizing radiation can be useful in the treatment of many different types of diseases. Radiation therapy uses targeted, high-dose radiation to indirectly or directly kill or stop the growth of cancer cells. The same mechanism of DNA damage is apparent in radiation therapy. Because cancer cells have a high mitotic rate, radiation can be very effective in cancer treatment. To put this into perspective, 1-2 Gy of radiation typically results in 40 double-stranded breaks in DNA. [3] External beam radiation is a form of radiation therapy that uses ionizing radiation to target tumors from sources outside of the body. Similar to the production of radiographic X-ray imaging, an electron is accelerated using a linear accelerator machine, and this results in the generation of photons. This external beam passes through the body, and energy is deposited throughout the path of the beam, targeting the primary tumor. Due to the damaging effects of radiation and the proximity of the tumor to surrounding healthy organs, these beams can be manipulated to minimize their damaging effects. [7] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction -- Physical and Biological Principles of Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic window in radiation also relies on differences in DNA interactions in a cancer cell versus those in a normal cell. Double-stranded DNA breaks caused by radiation result in a mitotic catastrophe where cell division is fatally interrupted; mitotic catastrophe is the main form of cell death induced by ionizing radiation. [1] Hence, the radiosensitivity of a cell line depends on its rate of cell division. In general, poorly differentiated tumor cells are more radiosensitive because a greater proportion of their cell population is dividing at any given time. This also applies to rapidly deviding normal tissue cells such as those of the gastrointestinal mucosa and explains why reactions such as mucositis and diarrhea can be common. The reliance of radiation effect on the cell cycle phase underlies one of the four basic tenets of radiation biology that dictate the success of a particular regimen: the redistribution of cells within the cell cycle, repair of DNA damage, repopulation of cells, and re-oxygenation of hypoxic areas within the tumor. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The negative effect on the human body is via cell damage. In most cases, the cell can repair itself, but the damage to core DNA and repair mechanisms can render the cell unable to operate normally. Cells die and can slough off the mucosa. A high radiation dose over a short span spread across multiple doses is thought to optimize cell repair than prolonged exposure at a low dose. Teratogenic effects are associated with ionizing radiation. Direct radiation, especially to a fetus in the first trimester, predisposes a high level of teratogenicity and may result in fetal death.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer development is always an issue of concern when it comes to radiation exposure. In both internal and external radiation, ionizing radiation can damage DNA indirectly or directly. Direct damage typically occurs with high ionizing particles such as alpha or beta particles. These particles can interact and transfer their energy directly to the cell's DNA, causing single-stranded or double-stranded breaks or chromosomal damage. Radiation can also cause damage indirectly by the formation of free radicals produced through ionization of surrounding water. This mechanism is present in about two-thirds of all radiation-induced cell damage. It is always important to note the increased risk of cancer development due to DNA mutations that occur with ionizing radiation. [3] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure Of Medical Imaging -- Issues of Concern -- Mechanism of Radiation-Associated Damage
retrieved_docs.content: Ionizing radiation affects the human body by causing damage at the atomic or molecular level, leading to cellular damage. They can cause damage to the vital organelles of cells, resulting in cell death, or they can damage human Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) either directly or indirectly. Direct effects occur when ionizing radiation comes directly in contact with molecules of DNA, leading to DNA strand breaks. Indirect effects are related to the ionization of molecules. Ionizing radiation causes the formation of hydroxyl ions at the cellular level by ionizing water molecules. These hydroxyl ions interact with DNA, leading to strand breaking or base damage. Adequate DNA repair mechanisms usually repair these DNA damages immediately, and if the damage cannot be repaired, these cells undergo apoptosis. The absence of DNA repair mechanisms or faulty repair of DNA leads to Genetic mutations that result in carcinoma formation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Repair -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: DNA repair is crucial to correcting the mistakes our replicative machinery makes when forming new materials within our bodies. Damage to DNA molecules, if left unrepaired, can often lead to carcinogenesis, which is the development of cancer cells in the affected area that can easily spread and replicate through mitosis. Conversely, cancer developments can be understood and analyzed through the mechanisms in which DNA damage occurs, and repair processes are affected as a result of the carcinogens in the body. This process has received extensive study relative to exogenous damage such as ionizing radiation that can lead to leukemia and melanoma due to various exposures, ranging from simple ultraviolet to X-ray.

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Effects of Radiation Therapy -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ionizing radiations generate free radicles, which subsequently damage vital cellular components and lead to double-stranded DNA breaks (DSBs), resulting in chromosomal aberrations and rearrangements. These lethally damaged cells may continue to divide a limited number of times before undergoing mitotic or apoptotic cell death, explaining the latency of acute side effects. Normal cells can repair DNA breaks better than tumor cells. Some cells undergo apoptosis due to the resulting damage, and some cells die during mitosis due to improperly repaired chromosomal damage. [5] Certain cell types, especially spermatogonia, serous cells in the salivary gland, and lymphocytes, undergo apoptosis during interphase after irradiation. [6] Some stem cells differentiate in response to irradiation and leave the reproductive pool, as seen in fibroblasts, which results in excessive fibrosis and scarring. [7] Moreover, irradiation activates various cellular signaling pathways, which lead to the expression and activation of proinflammatory, profibrotic cytokines, coagulation cascade, and vascular injury. [8] These changes contribute to edema, inflammatory responses, erythema in the skin, increased intracranial pressure in the central nervous system, and lung fibrosis. Moreover, some DSBs affecting cell cycle signaling and tumor suppressor genes can promote malignant transformation and subsequent malignancies 10-20 years after radiotherapy. Since radiotherapy requires oxygen-free radicles, it's essential to ensure adequate oxygen delivery to the tumor before radiotherapy e.g., treatment of anemia before radiotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Mutation -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Mutagen-induced: Mutagens alter the structure of DNA after direct contact and cause induced mutations either through sources of radiation or chemicals. The most common sources of radiation that act as mutagens include ultraviolet (UV) and ionizing radiation. [42] [43] Most skin cancers are caused due to UV light exposure, either from sunlight or artificial sources. UV light most frequently causes pyrimidine dimers and pyrimidine pyrimidone photoproducts, although other minor products are also formed, such as pyrimidine hydrate. These products, particularly the dimers, require TLS for replication to proceed, further adding to their potential for mutation. Furthermore, UV light can cause DNA adducts, DNA-protein crosslinks, and DNA strand breakages. [44] [45] Ionizing radiation, such as x-rays or gamma rays, causes DNA damage either directly through single-strand or double-strand breaks or indirectly through the production of ROS or other free radicals. [46] [47] The autosomal recessive condition xeroderma pigmentosum is associated with dysfunctional TLS, typically due to a deletion mutation. Affected individuals are at increased sensitivity to UV and ionizing radiation-induced genetic mutations with a significantly elevated risk of most forms of pre-malignant skin lesions as well as skin cancer. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Palliation Radiation Therapy of the Spinal Cord -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The principle underlying radiation therapy is to target malignant cells by hindering the cellular response to DNA damage and or replication. Radiation therapy is applied over the course of multiple treatments to reduce toxicity to the normal cells and target rapidly proliferating cells. Radiation acts in two ways: 1: To directly effectuate both single and double-stranded breaks to the DNA structure 2: To indirectly produce free radicals derived from the ionization of the water component of cells. [10] Radiation therapy also benefits from the activation of the NFκB transcription pathway, which increases the radiosensitivity of the cancer cells. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy -- Definition/Introduction -- Physical and Biological Principles of Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: The most common form of ionizing radiation used in clinical practice is the photon. However, electrons are also commonly used for increasing the radiation dose to the skin when needed. More exotic particles such as protons, carbon ions, or neutrons can be utilized for certain diagnoses or tumor locations but are available only in specialized centers. Each type of radiation possesses unique physical characteristics which dictate the type of interactions that will occur as it travels through the patient’s body. This, in turn, determines how and where the dose is deposited in the tissue, and knowledge of these patterns can be manipulated to limit the dose to normal structures and thereby improve the therapeutic window.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Safety and Protection -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: A basic understanding of the science behind the damaging effects of radiation is crucial in evaluating the different strategies to protect medical professionals and patients. X-rays are composed of high-energy photons within the electromagnetic spectrum. X-rays are notable in comparison to lower energy photons since they are powerful enough to break molecular bonds and ionize atoms. [4] This ionization produces free radicals, chemically active compounds that can indirectly damage DNA. [5] Medical staff and patients can be exposed to x-ray radiation either as scattered x-rays or by direct exposure to the x-ray beam. Scattered x-rays give up part of their energy during the scattering process, and thus energy deposited in tissues from scattered x-rays is lower than directly from the x-ray source. Radiation doses can be expressed in three different ways. The absorbed dose is the radiation deposited in an object and is measured in milligrays (mGy). The equivalent dose is calculated, taking into account the organ-specific radiation exposure, as well as the organ's sensitivity to radiation, and is expressed in millisieverts (mSv). The effective dose is the sum over the entire body of the individual organ equivalent doses and is expressed in millisieverts (mSv). An understanding of these definitions is critical to interpreting dose recommendations. The ICRP's dose recommendations are shown in fig 1. [3] For reference, 20 mSv/year roughly equates to 2 to 3 abdominal and pelvic computed tomography (CT) scans or 7 TO 9 years of background radiation. Exposure surpassing this threshold averaged over five years has been associated with a 1 in 1000 lifetime risk of fatal cancer. [6] [7] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinogenesis -- Causes -- Physical Carcinogens
retrieved_docs.content: Ultraviolet and ionizing radiation

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Effects of Radiation Therapy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Proton Beam Therapy - This treatment uses protons rather than x-rays. At high energy, protons can destroy cancer cells. The protons deposit the specific dose of radiation therapy to the targeted tissue. There is very little radiation dose beyond the tumor as compared to x-rays. This limits damage to nearby healthy tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation is detrimental to body tissues based on how quickly their cells turn over. Those with a high mitotic index will be more severely affected at a lower dose. These include bone marrow, the gastrointestinal tract, and skin.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Cancer Cell Cycle Phases -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In many cancer cells, there must be an issue on both alleles of a chromatid for cancer to occur. This is known as the two-hit hypothesis, and is the reason why cancer is often time heritable. Both alleles must be defective for cancer to occur; otherwise, the functioning allele will be favored, and cancer will be suppressed normally. Besides being passed down in genetics, environmental factors may also play a crucial role in developing cancer. Radical oxygens could lead to changes in the DNA and could lead to cancer if not corrected. Another common environmental factor that can damage DNA is UV light that radiates from the sun. This radiation can lead to the formation of thymidine dimers, which are normally fixed by nucleotide excision repair (NER); however, errors in this process can lead to misreading of the DNA and thus lead to cancer. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1376
retrieved_docs.content: indicating that each may act as a radioprotector, radiosensitizer, or have its activity altered by radiation exposure. Together, these relationships emphasize the dual considerations of therapeutic benefit, toxic risk, and radiation‑related effects, and support comprehensive patient care by prompting clinicians to assess drug‑radiation interactions and to select appropriate protective strategies during diagnostic or therapeutic radiation procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 27
retrieved_docs.content: radiation surgery (الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية). Overall, the hierarchy connects general radioactive‑isotope examinations to specific imaging and therapeutic techniques: transmission imaging and myocardial imaging derive from the broad radioactive‑isotope examination; nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures branch into radionuclide localization of tumor and radioisotope studies of the central nervous system; radionuclide imaging actions serve as methods for these procedures; and therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine link diagnostic imaging to radiation‑based treatments such as radiation therapy, intracavitary radiotherapy, and stereotactic radiation surgery. This integrated framework clarifies how diagnostic nuclear‑medicine modalities and therapeutic radiation practices are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient evaluation and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Radionuclide imaging – action is identified as a method of other nuclear medicine imaging, indicating that this imaging technique is employed as one of the various approaches within the broader field of nuclear medicine to visualize physiological and biochemical processes in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is considered a subdiscipline of radiation therapy, indicating that it falls under the broader field of radiation therapy, which encompasses the clinical use of ionizing radiation for treatment purposes, including both external beam radiotherapy and nuclear medicine techniques. This hierarchical relationship highlights that therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine are integral components of radiation therapy, contributing to the management of cancer and other conditions through targeted radiation-based interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: The action of radionuclide imaging is a method_of radionuclide localization of tumor, providing a technique to precisely locate tumors using radioactive tracers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2108
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging, therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, and الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) are all classified as sub‑domains of الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (examination using radioactive isotopes). In addition, therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is placed under radiation therapy, reflecting its role in both diagnostic imaging with radioactive tracers and therapeutic use of ionizing radiation. This hierarchical arrangement underscores the close connection between diagnostic nuclear imaging techniques such as transmission imaging and therapeutic modalities, including stereotactic radiosurgery, all of which share a common foundation in the application of radioactive isotopes and radiation physics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 322
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents characterized by a tetracyclic quinone structure and potent cytotoxic activity. Both aklarubicin and idarubicin are identified as members of this anthracycline class, sharing similar mechanisms of action that involve intercalation into DNA, inhibition of topoisomerase II, and generation of free radicals, which together contribute to their effectiveness in the treatment of various malignancies. Recognizing aklarubicin and idarubicin as part of the broader anthracycline group helps clinicians understand their pharmacologic properties, potential toxicities such as cardiotoxicity, and appropriate therapeutic use within oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 131
retrieved_docs.content: process إزالة السمية (removal of toxicity), is a therapeutic intervention with therapeutic intent. Musculoskeletal disorder therapy is related to المعالجة التأهيلية, indicating that rehabilitative approaches are applied in musculoskeletal conditions. Together, these structures illustrate how cancer‑directed chemical therapies, their administration methods, associated toxicities, maintenance chemo‑radiotherapy frameworks, and broader rehabilitative interventions such as detoxification and musculoskeletal therapy are organized within a comprehensive therapeutic hierarchy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: Unknown Cellular or Molecular Interaction [MoA] is characterized by having the mechanism of action of jodid draselný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1304
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that share a common anthracycline core and are used primarily in chemotherapy. Within this class, the drugs aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron are recognized as individual members. All three agents intercalate into DNA, inhibit topoisomerase II, and generate free radicals, mechanisms that contribute to their cytotoxic effects against rapidly dividing cancer cells. Because they belong to the same pharmacologic group, aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron exhibit similar therapeutic indications, such as treatment of leukemias and solid tumors, as well as comparable adverse effect profiles, most notably dose‑dependent cardiotoxicity and myelosuppression. Understanding that aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron are members of the Anthracyclines and related substances class helps clinicians anticipate their pharmacodynamics, manage shared toxicities, and make informed decisions when selecting or substituting agents within this chemotherapy category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: The community identifies a set of pharmacologic and biochemical agents—including HIV, antitumor antibiotics, bendroflumethiazid, diosmin, galantamin, glucose, gum karaya, Na‑K‑Cl symporter inhibitors, clotrimazole, vitamin B12, glycyrhetinic acid, nitric oxide, pentagastrin, and silymarin—as having documented interactions with ionizing radiation. These agents can alter radiation‑induced biological responses by modulating cellular protection, DNA repair, or radiosensitization, making them permissible qualifiers in radiation‑effect assessments. The same compounds are also recognized as relevant qualifiers within the domain of chemistry, reflecting their importance in chemical research, pharmaceutical formulation, and interdisciplinary study of infectious agents, pharmacology, and biochemistry. Understanding these relationships supports safe therapeutic use and informs patient care in contexts involving radiation exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances is a pharmacologic class that includes the anthracycline agents aklarubicin, epirubicin, and idarubicin. These three compounds share a common tetracyclic quinone structure and are employed as cytotoxic chemotherapy for a variety of malignancies, with similar mechanisms of action and overlapping safety considerations such as potential cardiotoxicity. The ET protocol is specifically mapped from epirubicin, indicating that epirubicin serves as the foundational drug or key component of that treatment regimen. Recognizing that aklarubicin, epirubicin, and idarubicin belong to the anthracycline class aids clinicians in understanding their therapeutic roles and risks, while the connection of epirubicin to the ET protocol informs the selection and design of appropriate chemotherapy schedules in patient care.
